section.title: Habakkuk's Prayer
section.sectionRationale: The book closes not with an argument but with a psalm. Having heard God's answer, Habakkuk turns his questions into worship: a prayer for mercy, a vision of God coming in awesome power to save His people, and a final confession of joy that holds even if every crop fails. It is the resolution of the whole dialogue, faith learning to sing in the dark.
section.thesis: Habakkuk answers God's word with worship: he prays for mercy in wrath, rehearses God's mighty acts of salvation, trembles at the coming judgment, and then rises to a settled joy in the LORD that does not depend on circumstances, though the fig tree does not bud.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the opening prayer, in wrath remember mercy; the vision of God marching out in glory to save His people and crush the wicked; the prophet's trembling as he waits; and the summit of the book, though the fig tree does not bud, yet I will rejoice in the God of my salvation.

verses.text: This is a prayer of Habakkuk the prophet, according to {{shigionoth|Shigionoth}}:
O LORD, I have heard the report of You; I stand in awe, O LORD, of Your deeds. Revive them in these years; make them known in these years. In Your wrath, remember mercy!

units.label: God comes in glory to save
units.note: a vision of the divine warrior

verses.text: God came from Teman, and the Holy One from Mount Paran. {{selah|Selah}} His glory covered the heavens, and His praise filled the earth.
His radiance was like the sunlight; rays flashed from His hand, where His power is hidden.
Plague went before Him, and fever followed in His steps.
He stood and measured the earth; He looked and startled the nations; the ancient mountains crumbled; the perpetual hills collapsed. His ways are everlasting.
I saw the tents of Cushan in distress; the curtains of Midian were trembling.
Were You angry at the rivers, O LORD? Was Your wrath against the streams? Did You rage against the sea when You rode on Your horses, on Your chariots of salvation?
You brandished Your bow; You called for many arrows. Selah You split the earth with rivers.
The mountains saw You and quaked; torrents of water swept by. The deep roared with its voice and lifted its hands on high.
Sun and moon stood still in their places at the flash of Your flying arrows, at the brightness of Your shining spear.
You marched across the earth with fury; You threshed the nations in wrath.
You went forth for the salvation of Your people, to save Your {{mashiach|anointed}}. You crushed the head of the house of the wicked and stripped him from head to toe. Selah
With his own spear You pierced his head, when his warriors stormed out to scatter us, gloating as though ready to secretly devour the weak.
You trampled the sea with Your horses, churning the great waters.
I heard and trembled within; my lips quivered at the sound. Decay entered my bones; I trembled where I stood. Yet I must wait patiently for the day of distress to come upon the people who invade us.

units.label: Joy though everything fails
units.note: yet I will rejoice in the LORD

verses.text: Though the fig tree does not bud and no fruit is on the vines, though the olive crop fails and the fields produce no food, though the sheep are cut off from the fold and no cattle are in the stalls,
yet I will exult in the LORD; I will rejoice in the God of my {{yeshuah|salvation}}!
GOD the Lord is my strength; He makes my feet like those of a deer; He makes me walk upon the heights! For the choirmaster. With stringed instruments.

shigionoth.gloss: A musical or liturgical term, probably indicating the tune, mood, or setting for the prayer, likely a passionate or wandering song. Its presence, with Selah and the closing note to the choirmaster, shows that chapter 3 is a psalm meant to be sung in the worship of God's people.

selah.gloss: A musical or literary marker found often in the Psalms, probably signaling a pause, an interlude, or a lifting up. It appears three times in this chapter, at verses 3, 9, and 13, inviting the worshiper to stop and weigh what has just been said about God's mighty acts.

mashiach.gloss: Anointed one, the one set apart with oil for God's service, especially the king. Here God goes forth to save His anointed and His people. The word is the root of Messiah and points beyond every earthly king to Christ, the true Anointed One whom God saves and vindicates.

yeshuah.gloss: Salvation, deliverance, rescue. The climax of Habakkuk's joy is that he rejoices in the God of his yeshuah. The word runs through the chapter, God rides on chariots of salvation and goes forth for the salvation of His people, and it is the root behind the name Jesus, the LORD saves.

basic.context: The final chapter is a prayer set to music, a psalm. Habakkuk has heard God's answers, and now he responds in worship. He asks God to act again in his own day and to remember mercy even in judgment. Then he paints a majestic picture of God coming in blazing power, the way He did at the exodus and Sinai, to rescue His people and crush the wicked. The vision leaves the prophet shaking. But he ends on the highest note in the book: even if every crop fails and every animal is gone, he will still rejoice in God his Savior.
basic.hermeneutics: This chapter shows what to do with God's answers: turn them into worship. The prayer draws on Israel's memory of God's past acts of salvation to build confidence for the future. The famous ending is not naive optimism; it is faith that has counted the cost of total loss and still chooses joy in God Himself. It teaches that our joy can rest on who God is rather than on what we have.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter is marked as a psalm: it is set according to Shigionoth, it uses Selah three times, and it ends with a note to the choirmaster. God goes forth to save His mashiach, His anointed, a word that points to the Messiah. And the whole prayer rises to yeshuah, salvation, the root behind the name Jesus: Habakkuk rejoices in the God of his salvation.
basic.typology: God riding out on chariots of salvation, crushing the head of the house of the wicked, echoes the promise of Genesis 3:15 and points to Christ who crushes the serpent. God going forth to save His anointed points to the Father raising and vindicating His Anointed One, Jesus. And the joy that survives total loss anticipates the resurrection hope that nothing can take away.
basic.christ: God saving His anointed and crushing the head of the wicked foreshadows the death and resurrection of Christ, in whom the serpent's head is crushed. The God of my salvation, yeshuah, is fully revealed in Jesus, whose very name means the LORD saves. And the joy of verse 18, held in the face of ruin, is the joy Paul echoes when he says rejoice in the Lord always, a joy grounded in the risen Christ.
basic.god: We learn that God is holy, gloriously powerful, and terrifying to His enemies, yet He comes for the salvation of His people. He is both the God of wrath and the God who is entreated to remember mercy. He is the strength of the weak, turning trembling knees into a deer's sure feet on the heights.
basic.crossReferences: Deuteronomy 33:2 and Judges 5 recall God coming from the south in glory. Genesis 3:15 lies behind crushing the head of the wicked. Philippians 4:4 and Romans 8:37 echo joy and triumph in God regardless of circumstances.
basic.oneStory: The book that began in protest ends in praise. Habakkuk learns to rest not on his circumstances but on the God of his salvation, whose past rescues guarantee the future and point to the ultimate rescue in Christ. His closing song is the voice of faith in every age, singing in the dark because the LORD is its strength.

context.literary: Chapter 3 is a self-contained psalm, complete with a superscription, the term Shigionoth, three uses of Selah, and a closing musical instruction to the choirmaster. It moves from petition, 3:2, to a theophany or vision of God the divine warrior, 3:3 to 15, to the prophet's trembling response, 3:16, and finally to a summit of confident joy, 3:17 to 19.
context.historical: The vision of God coming from Teman and Paran deliberately recalls the exodus, Sinai, and the conquest, when God marched from the south to save Israel and shake the nations. Habakkuk prays these ancient rescues as the ground for trusting God in the coming Babylonian crisis, when fields and flocks would indeed be devastated.
context.canonical: As a psalm embedded in a prophetic book, chapter 3 joins the language of Israel's worship to the message of the prophets. Its imagery of the divine warrior links back to the Song of the Sea and the blessing of Moses, and its crushing of the head of the wicked reaches back to Genesis 3:15 and forward to the victory of Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To turn God's revealed answer into worship: to plead for mercy, to rehearse God's mighty acts of salvation as grounds for trust, and to arrive at a joy in God that no loss can destroy, teaching God's people to sing in the face of coming judgment.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The theophany describes God's past and future acts of salvation. The closing confession, 3:17 to 19, is prescriptive: it models the response every believer is called to, rejoicing in the God of our salvation even when everything else fails.

debates.question: Is chapter 3 a vision of a future deliverance, a remembrance of past acts, or both?

views.label: A rehearsal of past salvation
views.case: The imagery of Teman, Paran, Cushan, and Midian recalls the exodus, Sinai, and conquest; Habakkuk remembers what God has done to build faith for the future.

views.label: A vision of God's coming action
views.case: The prayer asks God to revive His work in these years, so the theophany also anticipates God's future coming in judgment and salvation, including the final day.

debates.bearing: The two hold together: because God has saved like this before, His people can trust Him to save again, ultimately in Christ and at the last day.

debates.question: Who is the anointed God goes forth to save in 3:13?

views.label: The king or the people of God
views.case: The anointed can mean the Davidic king or, corporately, God's covenant people whom He rescues from the wicked oppressor.

views.label: Ultimately the Messiah
views.case: The word mashiach points beyond every earthly king to Christ, the true Anointed One, whom God vindicates and through whom He crushes the head of the wicked.

debates.bearing: Whether read of the king, the people, or the Messiah, the verse celebrates that God acts to save His anointed, a hope that finds its fullness in Jesus.

study.originalLanguages: Chapter 3 announces itself as a psalm: it is set according to Shigionoth, a musical direction; it is punctuated three times by Selah, a call to pause and reflect; and it closes with a note for the choirmaster with stringed instruments. At its heart God goes forth for the salvation of His people, to save His mashiach, His anointed, the word that becomes Messiah. The prophet's confidence rises to its peak on yeshuah, salvation: though everything is stripped away, he will rejoice in the God of his salvation, the very root that stands behind the name Jesus, the LORD saves.

typology.type: God crushing the head of the house of the wicked
typology.fulfillment: Christ crushing the head of the serpent, Genesis 3:15 and Romans 16:20, decisively at the cross and resurrection.

typology.type: God going forth to save His anointed
typology.fulfillment: The Father vindicating and raising His Anointed One, Jesus the Messiah, and in Him saving all His people.

typology.type: Joy that survives the loss of everything
typology.fulfillment: The resurrection joy in Christ that no circumstance can steal, Philippians 4:4 and Romans 8:37 to 39.

study.christ: The divine warrior who marches out on chariots of salvation to crush the head of the house of the wicked and to save His anointed foreshadows Christ, who crushes the serpent's head and is Himself the Anointed One whom God raises and vindicates. The refrain of salvation, yeshuah, finds its fullness in the name of Jesus, the LORD saves. And Habakkuk's closing joy, held fast though the fig tree does not bud, is the joy of the gospel: because God is our salvation in Christ, we can rejoice even when everything else is stripped away, for nothing can separate us from the love of God.
study.god: God is the Holy One of dazzling glory, before whom mountains crumble and nations tremble, whose ways are everlasting. He is a God of real wrath against evil, yet He can be entreated to remember mercy, and He comes above all for the salvation of His people. He is the strength of the weak, giving trembling knees the sure footing of a deer on the heights, so that even in devastation His people can walk and sing.

crossReferences.note: The LORD came from Sinai and dawned from Seir and Paran, the exodus imagery Habakkuk draws on for the theophany.
The promise to crush the serpent's head, echoed in crushing the head of the house of the wicked.
Rejoice in the Lord always, the New Testament echo of joy grounded in God rather than circumstances.
More than conquerors, and nothing can separate us from God's love, the fullness of Habakkuk's fig tree joy.

study.oneStory: The book that opened with a cry of protest ends with a song of trust. Habakkuk learns to rest his joy not on his harvest but on the God of his salvation, whose past rescues assure the future and point ahead to the ultimate rescue in Christ, who crushes the serpent and saves His anointed. His final confession is the anthem of faith in every generation: though everything fails, yet I will rejoice in the LORD.

sermon.title: I Will Rejoice!
sermon.why: From Begg's series A Study in Habakkuk, this message expounds Habakkuk 3:1-19, the prophet's closing prayer and his triumphant joy in the God of his salvation even if the fig tree does not bud. A faithful, orthodox handling of this exact movement.

quiz.q: What kind of writing is Habakkuk 3?

options.text: A prayer set to music, a psalm, marked by Shigionoth, Selah, and a note to the choirmaster.
options.note: The chapter is a sung prayer, turning God's answers into worship.

options.text: A prayer of praise responding in worship to God's answer.
options.note: True to its content, though it misses that it is specifically a psalm set for music.

options.text: A list of rules for temple sacrifices.
options.note: It is worship, but a sung prayer, not a manual of sacrificial law.

options.text: A legal contract between Habakkuk and Babylon.
options.note: It is a psalm of prayer and praise, not a contract.

quiz.q: What does Habakkuk ask for in verse 2?

options.text: That God would revive His work in these years and, in His wrath, remember mercy.
options.note: He prays for God to act again and to temper judgment with mercy.

options.text: That God would show mercy even in the midst of the coming judgment.
options.note: This is half his prayer; he also asks God to revive His mighty work in their day.

options.text: That the Babylonians would be spared all judgment.
options.note: He asks mercy for God's people; the oppressor remains under judgment.

options.text: That God would destroy Judah completely and without mercy.
options.note: He pleads for mercy in the midst of wrath, not for total destruction.

quiz.q: What does the vision in verses 3 to 15 portray?

options.text: God coming in awesome glory as a warrior to save His people and crush the wicked.
options.note: The theophany recalls the exodus and Sinai, God marching out for salvation.

options.text: God's overwhelming power over creation and the nations.
options.note: True, but it leaves out the purpose: He marches out to save His people.

options.text: A description of Habakkuk's travels.
options.note: The place names evoke God's past acts of salvation, not the prophet's journeys.

options.text: The Babylonians triumphing over God.
options.note: God crushes the head of the house of the wicked; He is not defeated.

quiz.q: What is the summit of the book in verses 17 to 18?

options.text: Though every crop and flock fails, yet Habakkuk will rejoice in the God of his salvation.
options.note: Joy grounded in God Himself, not in circumstances, is the book's climax.

options.text: Choosing to find joy in God even in the face of total loss.
options.note: A faithful paraphrase, though the striking picture of the failed fig tree and empty stalls is the point.

options.text: A demand that God explain everything first.
options.note: He moves past demanding answers to resting in God's character.

options.text: A promise that the harvest will always be abundant.
options.note: He rejoices precisely when the harvest fails, resting in God alone.

quiz.q: How does the salvation of God's anointed in 3:13 point to Christ?

options.text: The word anointed, mashiach, points beyond every king to the Messiah, and crushing the head of the wicked echoes the promise of Genesis 3:15.
options.note: God saving His anointed and crushing the wicked finds its fullness in Christ's victory.

options.text: It celebrates God going forth to save and vindicate His anointed one.
options.note: True, and the word mashiach and the head-crushing draw the line specifically to Christ.

options.text: It teaches that the Messiah would be a Babylonian king.
options.note: The anointed is God's own, saved and vindicated, fulfilled in Jesus.

options.text: It has no connection to Christ whatsoever.
options.note: Mashiach is the root of Messiah, and the head-crushing echoes Genesis 3:15.

questions: Habakkuk turned God's answers into worship rather than just information. How might you make praise, not only understanding, your response to what God has shown you?
The prophet built his confidence by remembering God's past acts of salvation. What specific rescues in your own life, or in the gospel, can you rehearse to steady your faith today?
In wrath, remember mercy was his prayer. Where do you need to ask God for mercy in the middle of a hard season, for yourself or for others?
Habakkuk chose joy in God even imagining total loss, no figs, no crops, no flocks. What would it look like for your joy to rest on God Himself rather than on your circumstances?
God made the trembling prophet's feet like a deer's on the heights. Where do you feel unsteady right now, and how might God's strength carry you through it?

translationNotes.note: Shigionoth is probably a musical term indicating the tune or mood for the prayer; it marks chapter 3 as a psalm for worship.
Selah, also at verses 9 and 13, is probably a musical or literary term signaling a pause or interlude for reflection.
Anointed translates mashiach, the root of Messiah; it can denote the king or the covenant people, and points ultimately to Christ.
The closing line, for the choirmaster with stringed instruments, is a liturgical direction confirming the chapter was sung in Israel's worship.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Habakkuk 3:1-19. Poetic lines rendered as flowing prose within each verse; Selah retained inline as in the source. No em or en dashes used.
